title: P3 Variables, Conditionals, and Stateful Program Behavior
short: computer science middle-grade node for variables, conditionals, and stateful program behavior
purpose: The learner explains how stored values, branching logic, and changing state affect program behavior without treating variables as labels only or conditionals as random forks.
exit: uses variables and conditionals meaningfully in a bounded program
explains how a change in stored state changes later program behavior
predicts which branch a conditional will follow in a bounded case
succeeds across more than one stateful programming context
passes a delayed recheck after spacing
evidence: describe what a variable stands for and how it changes
trace a simple conditional outcome from the stated condition
connect program state to later outputs or actions
avoid treating stored values and displayed labels as interchangeable
failure: variable-as-name only
learner treats a variable as a label only and not as a changing stored value.
conditional-branch confusion
learner cannot determine which path a bounded conditional should follow.
state-change opacity
learner does not connect updated state to later behavior in the program.
trace-fragility
learner can reason through one familiar stateful example only and cannot transfer to another bounded case.
interventions: state-tracing routines that record variable values step by step
if-then branch walkthroughs with explicit true and false pathway comparisons
before-and-after state tables connecting updates to later outputs
paired transfer tasks using the same state-and-branch structure in a second program context
items: Why is a variable more than just a word in a program?
A variable stores a value that can be used and changed, so it affects how the program behaves over time.
How can you tell which branch an if statement will follow?
You compare the current program state or value with the condition and determine whether the condition is true or false.
Why should you track a value after it changes?
Because later parts of the program may use the updated value, which can change outputs, choices, or actions.
noteSecure: Explains variables, conditionals, and state changes with stable trace and branch reasoning.
noteEmerging: Can copy stateful code patterns but still loses track of variable meaning and branch outcomes.

title: A2 Algorithm Design, Efficiency Awareness, and Decomposition Discipline
short: computer science middle-grade node for algorithm design, efficiency awareness, and decomposition discipline
purpose: The learner designs algorithms with explicit decomposition and begins comparing solution quality without treating all correct-looking procedures as equally effective.
exit: designs a bounded algorithm with explicit ordered subproblems
compares two bounded solutions using correctness and simple efficiency awareness
explains why a decomposition helps manage complexity
succeeds across more than one algorithmic problem context
passes a delayed recheck after spacing
evidence: break a problem into stable subproblems before writing the full procedure
compare two procedures using at least one concrete quality criterion
distinguish correctness from efficiency or clarity in a bounded case
avoid treating a longer or shorter procedure as automatically better
failure: correctness-efficiency collapse
learner confuses a correct algorithm with the most effective one without comparison criteria.
decomposition-drift
learner breaks a problem into parts that do not fit together coherently.
criterion-free comparison
learner judges one solution as better without naming a bounded reason.
near-transfer breakdown
learner can design for one exact case only and cannot adapt the structure to a nearby problem.
interventions: algorithm comparison tasks with explicit correctness, step-count, and clarity criteria
subproblem-mapping routines that require each part to connect to the whole solution
why-this-solution-is-better prompts tied to one named criterion
near-transfer redesign tasks that preserve structure while changing a bounded task detail
items: Why can two correct algorithms still differ in quality?
They can differ in efficiency, clarity, or how well they manage the task, even if both eventually work.
What makes a decomposition useful rather than random?
A useful decomposition creates subproblems that fit together coherently and support the larger solution.
Why should you name a criterion when comparing two solutions?
A criterion makes the comparison disciplined by showing what feature of the algorithm you are actually judging.
noteSecure: Designs and compares bounded algorithms with explicit decomposition and basic efficiency awareness.
noteEmerging: Can produce solutions but still compares them loosely and decomposes problems inconsistently.

title: C2 Debugging Strategy, Trace Reasoning, and Test Cases
short: computer science middle-grade node for debugging strategy, trace reasoning, and test cases
purpose: The learner traces program behavior, isolates errors, and uses simple test cases intentionally without treating debugging as repeated guessing.
exit: traces a bounded program to identify likely error locations
uses a simple test case intentionally to check program behavior
revises code or logic with a stated debugging reason
succeeds across more than one bounded debugging context
passes a delayed recheck after spacing
evidence: trace values and control flow through a simple program
choose or explain a test case that checks a specific behavior
separate bug location from bug repair in a bounded case
avoid random code changes without a stated hypothesis
failure: guess-and-change debugging
learner changes code repeatedly without tracing or a bounded hypothesis.
test-case-without-purpose
learner uses examples without explaining what behavior they are checking.
trace-breakdown
learner loses track of values or control flow while following the program.
fix-without-verification
learner revises code but does not check whether the target bug was actually resolved.
interventions: trace tables that record values and branch choices line by line
test-purpose prompts that require naming what a chosen input is supposed to check
bug-location-before-fix routines that separate diagnosis from repair
after-the-fix checks comparing expected and actual behavior explicitly
items: Why is changing code randomly a weak debugging strategy?
It removes evidence about the cause of the bug and makes it hard to tell which change mattered.
What makes a test case useful?
A useful test case is chosen to check a specific behavior or boundary in the program, not just to try any input.
Why should you verify a fix after changing the code?
Because the change may not resolve the original problem or may introduce a new one, so the result must be checked against the target behavior.
noteSecure: Uses tracing and test cases intentionally to debug bounded programs with stated reasons for revisions.
noteEmerging: Can notice bugs and make changes but still debugs by guesswork and weak verification.

title: R3 Data Transformation, Tables, and Computational Models
short: computer science middle-grade node for data transformation, tables, and computational models
purpose: The learner transforms and analyzes data using structured tables, rules, or beginner computational models without treating transformed output as meaningful unless the rule and representation are understood.
exit: applies a bounded transformation rule to organized data
uses a table or simple model to explain how data changed
interprets the resulting representation with reference to the transformation process
succeeds across more than one bounded data-transformation context
passes a delayed recheck after spacing
evidence: track what rule or operation was applied to the data
explain how a transformed table differs from the original data organization
connect the output representation back to the transformation process
avoid treating automated output as self-explanatory
failure: output-without-rule meaning
learner reads transformed output without understanding the rule that produced it.
table-column drift
learner loses track of what each column or transformed field represents.
model-as-fact
learner treats a simple computational model as raw reality instead of a rule-based representation.
transformation-transfer fragility
learner understands one familiar transformation only and cannot adapt to a nearby case.
interventions: before-and-after table comparisons that label the transformation rule explicitly
column-meaning checks requiring each data field to be interpreted in context
model-versus-original-data routines that distinguish representation from source information
near-transfer transformation tasks that preserve the same rule in a second bounded dataset
items: Why is a transformed data table not self-explanatory?
Because you need to know what rule or process created the new values before the output can be interpreted correctly.
How can a column in a table become confusing after a transformation?
A column can become confusing if its meaning changes and the learner does not track what the new values represent.
Why should a computational model be treated as a representation?
Because it applies a bounded rule or structure to data and should be interpreted with its assumptions and limits in mind.
noteSecure: Explains data transformations and simple computational models with stable rule and representation tracking.
noteEmerging: Can produce transformed outputs but still overtrusts tables or models without understanding how they were formed.

title: N2 Network Pathways, Protocol Ideas, and System Dependence
short: computer science middle-grade node for network pathways, protocol ideas, and system dependence
purpose: The learner explains how information moves through networks and why shared rules matter without reducing networking to a vague idea that devices simply "connect somehow."
exit: describes a bounded pathway for information movement through a networked system
explains why shared communication rules or protocol ideas matter
recognizes that networked tasks depend on multiple system parts and agreed behaviors
succeeds across more than one bounded networking context
passes a delayed recheck after spacing
evidence: identify at least one route or pathway through which information moves
explain why a shared rule is needed for communication to work
distinguish a network path from one local device action
avoid treating communication as if information appears instantly without system structure
failure: network-as-magic belief
learner treats network communication as unexplained appearance rather than movement through system parts.
protocol-purpose weakness
learner cannot explain why shared communication rules matter.
pathway-fragmentation
learner knows pieces of a networked task but not how the parts relate.
context-bound networking
learner understands one familiar example only and cannot transfer to another bounded connected-system case.
interventions: send-and-receive pathway diagrams that name system parts and movement steps
shared-rule comparison tasks showing what happens when system parts do and do not follow the same rule
local-versus-networked scenario sorts with explicit reasoning prompts
paired transfer practice across two simple communication-system examples
items: Why is it not enough to say devices are just connected?
Because a strong explanation shows how information moves through system parts and not just that a connection exists.
What is the purpose of a shared communication rule?
A shared rule helps system parts send, receive, and interpret information in a compatible way.
How is a networked task different from a local task?
A networked task depends on multiple connected parts and agreed behaviors, not just one device acting alone.
noteSecure: Explains network pathways and shared communication rules with stable system-dependence reasoning.
noteEmerging: Knows devices connect but still treats network communication as opaque or underspecified.

title: N3 Security Risk, Access Control, and Privacy Foundations
short: computer science middle-grade node for security risk, access control, and privacy foundations
purpose: The learner reasons about basic security risks, privacy, and protective computing practices without reducing security to rule recitation only.
exit: identifies a bounded security or privacy risk in a simple digital scenario
explains how an access-control or protective practice addresses that risk
distinguishes sharing, access, and privacy decisions in bounded cases
succeeds across more than one bounded security or privacy context
passes a delayed recheck after spacing
evidence: identify who should and should not have access in a simple scenario
connect a protective practice to the risk it is meant to reduce
use privacy reasoning that goes beyond rule repetition alone
avoid assuming that being online automatically means information should be public
failure: security-rule parroting
learner repeats a rule without explaining what risk it addresses.
access-boundary confusion
learner cannot distinguish authorized from unauthorized access in a bounded case.
privacy-public collapse
learner treats shared digital spaces as if all information should be openly accessible.
risk-practice disconnect
learner names a protective action but cannot connect it to a specific risk reduction.
interventions: risk-and-protection matching tasks that pair threats with bounded protective practices
authorized-versus-unauthorized access scenario sorts with explicit reasoning
privacy-boundary prompts that compare public, shared, and private cases
why-this-protection helps explanations tied to one concrete digital risk
items: Why is a security rule weak if you cannot explain what risk it addresses?
Because strong security reasoning connects the rule or practice to the specific kind of problem it is meant to reduce.
How can access control help protect information?
It helps by limiting who can reach or change information so unauthorized use becomes harder.
Why is shared information not always public information?
Because some information is meant for a bounded group and should not be treated as open to everyone.
noteSecure: Explains simple security and privacy practices through explicit risk and access-control reasoning.
noteEmerging: Knows some safety rules but still reasons weakly about risk, access, and privacy boundaries.

title: I3 Computing Impacts, Bias, and Human-Centered Evaluation
short: computer science middle-grade node for computing impacts, bias, and human-centered evaluation
purpose: The learner critiques computing systems in terms of fairness, impact, and human use contexts without treating technology as automatically neutral or automatically harmful.
exit: identifies a bounded impact of a computing system on people or groups
recognizes that data, design, or context choices can affect fairness
uses a simple human-centered criterion to evaluate a computing decision or tool
succeeds across more than one bounded computing-impact context
passes a delayed recheck after spacing
evidence: describe at least one way a computing system can affect different people differently
connect a design or data choice to a potential fairness issue
evaluate a tool or decision using a named human-centered criterion
avoid reducing computing impacts to praise or blame without bounded analysis
failure: technology-neutrality assumption
learner assumes computing systems have no built-in impact from data or design choices.
fairness-without-cause
learner names fairness as a concern but cannot connect it to a system feature or data choice.
one-sided impact reasoning
learner evaluates a system only as good or bad without bounded tradeoff analysis.
human-context omission
learner discusses a tool without considering who uses it and under what conditions.
interventions: people-and-system comparison tasks that identify who is helped or harmed in bounded cases
data-choice and design-choice prompts tied to fairness outcomes
tradeoff evaluation routines that require one benefit and one risk to be named
human-centered design checks that ask who the user is and what conditions matter
items: Why can a computing system affect people differently?
Because design choices, data, and usage contexts can create different outcomes for different users or groups.
How can a data choice create a fairness issue?
A data choice can create fairness issues if it leaves out important people, reflects bias, or leads the system to treat groups unevenly.
Why is calling a system simply good or bad not enough?
Because strong evaluation should consider bounded benefits, risks, and who is affected rather than giving a one-word judgment.
noteSecure: Evaluates computing impacts with explicit fairness, user-context, and tradeoff reasoning.
noteEmerging: Can discuss digital impacts but still treats fairness and harm in broad unsupported terms.
